regions: Parental ranges: Western Indian Ocean / Red Sea / Persian Gulf
countries: Captive-bred (Sea & Reef Aquaculture, ORA, and similar facilities)
nativeRange.habitat: Does not occur in the wild — this fish is a captive-bred designer hybrid produced by aquaculture facilities by crossing Pseudochromis springeri (Springer's Dottyback, native to the Red Sea) with Pseudochromis aldabraensis (Neon / Orange Dottyback, native to the western Indian Ocean including Aldabra, the Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka). In the wild the parent species inhabit coral and rocky reefs at roughly 1–25 m depth.

diet.notes: Generally considered an unfussy micropredator that has been reported to readily accept a wide range of meaty foods, including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, finely chopped seafood, and quality marine pellet or flake preparations. Has been documented preying on bristleworms in the display, which is often regarded as a useful side effect. Generally advised to feed 1–2 small meals per day with varied carnivore-formulated foods.

summary: The Black Neon Dottyback is a captive-bred designer hybrid — a cross between Pseudochromis springeri and P. aldabraensis — known for its shifting black-to-rust body and vivid electric-blue dorsal striping. Generally considered hardy and beginner-friendly, it is semi-aggressive and territorial and best kept one per tank with ample rockwork.
careNotes:
The Black Neon Dottyback is a captive-bred designer hybrid produced by crossing Pseudochromis springeri (Springer's Dottyback, a Red Sea endemic) with Pseudochromis aldabraensis (the Neon or Orange Dottyback, native to the western Indian Ocean). It does not occur in the wild and is offered in the trade primarily by Sea & Reef Aquaculture, ORA, and a handful of similar facilities. The body colour shifts between near-jet-black and a rust-orange depending on mood and lighting, with double electric-blue stripes running the length of the dorsal fin and into the caudal fin — combining the facial banding of the springeri parent with the body striping of the aldabraensis parent.

Care is generally considered easy and well-suited to beginners: the species adapts readily to captive foods, tolerates the standard reef parameter window, and is reliably available as a tank-bred specimen. Conspecific aggression tends to be high; keep only one per tank and do not combine with other dottybacks.

The Black Neon Dottyback is generally considered reef-compatible with corals and most sessile invertebrates. Like its parents, it has been reported to hunt small ornamental shrimp and other small mobile invertebrates, so caution is warranted in tanks with prized miniature crustaceans. A tightly sealed lid is generally advised — dottybacks have been widely reported to jump from open tanks, particularly when spooked or chased. Plenty of live rock with numerous caves, crevices, and overhangs is required to give the fish a defendable home territory.

A precise lifespan figure is not available for this hybrid; the 5–8 year estimate draws on general Pseudochromidae longevity and should be treated as approximate.
breedingNotes: Breeding the F1 hybrid in home aquaria is not commonly reported, and any offspring would not breed true. The parent species P. springeri and P. aldabraensis have both been bred in captivity by commercial aquaculture facilities (the Black Neon hybrid line itself is maintained by Sea & Reef Aquaculture and similar facilities). Both parents are cave-spawning species in which the male prepares a crevice and courts a female who deposits an egg ball inside; rearing fry is generally considered moderately challenging.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for confirmed hybrid origin (Pseudochromis aldabraensis x P. springeri), care level (Easy), temperament (Semi-Aggressive), reef compatible (Yes), diet (carnivore — varied meaty/carnivore foods), max size (3 in), minimum tank (30 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), and the colour-shifting (jet-black to rust) body / Springer face-band + Aldabraensis blue-stripe appearance description. Note: the LiveAquaria page lists family as 'Labridae' which is incorrect for Pseudochromidae; corrected from FishBase data on the parent species.

additional.name: Sea & Reef Aquaculture
additional.notes: Origin of the Black Neon line and second confirming source. Used for max size (3.5 in / ~9 cm), confirmed hybrid parents (P. springeri x P. aldabraensis), temperament/aggression context ("captive-bred Dottybacks tend to be much less aggressive than their wild collected counterpart"), guidance to keep singly unless aquarium is very large, recommendation to add last, and the body colour description (rust to jet-black, double electric-blue dorsal/caudal band).

additional.name: FishBase (Pseudochromis springeri)
additional.notes: Used for the springeri parent's family (Pseudochromidae) and order (Ovalentaria/misc) — corrects the LiveAquaria 'Labridae' error — plus parent native range (Red Sea), parent max size 5.5 cm TL, and wild temperature range 24–27°C.

additional.name: FishBase (Pseudochromis aldabraensis)
additional.notes: Used for the aldabraensis parent's native range (Aldabra, Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, Pakistan, Sri Lanka), parent max size 10 cm TL, depth 1–25 m, wild temperature range 24–28°C, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: BiomesInABox (Neon Dottyback care guide)
additional.notes: Third confirming source for water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.025) and tank minimum (30 gal). Source for the high-escapeRisk rating: explicitly notes 'Neon Dottybacks may jump out of the aquarium when spooked' and recommends a well-fitted lid. Confirms severe conspecific aggression.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding the F1 hybrid in home aquaria is not commonly reported, and any offspring would not breed true. Both parent species have been bred in captivity by commercial facilities, and the Black Neon hybrid line itself is maintained by aquaculture operations. Both parents are cave-spawning species in which the male prepares a crevice and courts a female who deposits an egg ball inside; raising larvae is considered moderately challenging.
